originalTitle: The cash-in-the-sofa saga that just won't go away for South Africa's president
title: The cash-in-the-sofa saga that just won't go away for South Africa's president
content: A committee of MPs has been formed to look at the case against Cyril Ramaphosa, but he may survive a vote.

originalTitle: Nearly 50 people die of thirst in Sahara desert after lorry breaks down
title: Important Lessons from the Big Hot Sahara Desert
content:
Hi there, little friends! It's your friendly reporter from Africa. Today, I have a story that is a bit sad, but it teaches us very important lessons about staying safe and being careful. Let's talk about the amazing continent of Africa and one of its biggest places, the Sahara Desert.

The Big Hot Sahara Desert

Africa is home to many amazing places. One of the biggest and most famous is the Sahara Desert. It is a very, very large place. Imagine a world with lots and lots of sand, no trees, and very little water. That's what the Sahara Desert looks like. It gets super hot during the day. The sun shines very brightly, and the sand feels like a hot oven. Did you know? The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the whole world! It's bigger than many countries put together!

A Long Trip in a Lorry

Sometimes, people need to travel across this big desert. They might go to visit family or for work. They usually travel in big trucks. We call these big trucks "lorries" here in Africa and other places. A lorry is like a very, very large car. It can carry many people and lots of things, like food or supplies. Did you know? Lorries are built strong so they can drive over bumpy roads and sand in the desert.

When Things Went Wrong

One day, a lorry was travelling through the Sahara Desert. It was carrying many people on its long journey. But then, something went very wrong. The lorry broke down. "Broke down" means it stopped working. Imagine your toy car suddenly stops moving, and you can't fix it right away. That's what happened to the big lorry. It was stuck in the middle of the huge, hot desert. Far away from any towns or help.

No Water, Big Thirst

When the lorry broke down, the people on board had a very big problem. They had no water left to drink. The desert is very, very hot. When it's hot, your body needs lots of water to stay cool and healthy. "Thirst" means you really, really need water. Your mouth feels dry, and your body feels weak. Without water, people can get very sick. It's one of the most important things for us to drink every day. Did you know? Your body is made up of mostly water! That's why we must drink water every day to stay strong and play.

Two Brave People

Amidst all the trouble, two people from the lorry were very brave. They knew they needed to find help. They decided to walk. They started a very, very long walk across the desert. This is called "trekking." They walked more than 50 kilometres. Can you imagine how far that is? It's like walking for many, many hours or even days! They walked and walked, even though they were tired and thirsty. They walked to find someone who could help all the other people. They wanted to tell the "authorities." Authorities are like the police or rescue teams. They are the people who help others in emergencies.

Finding Help

Imagine how hard it must have been for these two brave people. The sun was hot, and they had little water. But they kept going, step by step. They knew other people were waiting and needed help very much. Finally, after their very long journey, they found people! They told them about the broken lorry and all the people stuck in the desert. The authorities quickly sent out help. They wanted to find the lorry and bring water and rescue everyone.

A Sad Ending for Many

But for many of the people on the lorry, help did not arrive quickly enough. The desert is so vast and finding things there can be hard. It is very sad to share this, but nearly 50 people died because they ran out of water and could not get help in time. This is a very sad event, and it makes us feel very sorry for all the people who were lost and their families.

What We Can Learn

This sad story teaches us some very important things. First, water is super important. Always carry enough water, especially when travelling to hot places. Second, always tell someone where you are going, especially if it's a long journey. This way, if something goes wrong, people know where to look for you. Third, it's good to travel with people who know the way and who are prepared for challenges. Being prepared helps a lot. Did you know? Some desert animals, like camels, can go a very long time without water, but humans cannot! We need water often.

Being Kind and Safe

This story reminds us to always be careful and think about safety first, especially when going on big adventures. We should also remember to be kind and thoughtful to everyone around us. We never know when someone might need help. Let's all think about the people who travel and hope they have safe journeys.

Remembering the People

We remember the nearly 50 people who lost their lives in the Sahara Desert. We send our thoughts and love to their families. This story is a powerful reminder of how precious water is and why we must always value it. It also reminds us how important it is for us to help each other when times are tough. So, my friends, stay safe, drink your water, and always be kind. See you next time!
summary: Nearly 50 people sadly died of thirst in the very hot Sahara Desert after their big truck broke down, but two brave people walked a long way to find help and tell the rescue teams.
whyMatters: This sad story matters because it teaches us how important it is to always have enough water, be prepared for trips, and remember that our bodies need water to stay alive and healthy.

originalTitle: Fall in official Ebola numbers appears to be good news but it's not that simple
title: Fall in official Ebola numbers appears to be good news but it's not that simple
content: There are now 380 confirmed cases of Ebola in DR Congo, far lower than initial estimates of suspected cases, writes Fergus Walsh.
